title: C++ References
content:
### References

A reference is an alias for another variable. Once initialized, a reference cannot be made to refer to a different variable.

---

### Syntax

```cpp
int original = 42;
int& ref = original;  // ref is a reference to original

ref = 100;            // Modifies original
cout << original;     // 100
```

---

### Reference vs Pointer

| Aspect | Reference | Pointer |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| Initialization | Must be initialized | Can be uninitialized |
| Reassignment | Cannot be reseated | Can point to different objects |
| Null state | Cannot be null | Can be nullptr |
| Dereference | Automatic | Requires `*` |
| Arithmetic | No pointer arithmetic | Supported |
| Use case | Pass/return by reference | Dynamic memory, optional params |

---

### References as Parameters

```cpp
void swap(int& a, int& b) {
  int temp = a;
  a = b;
  b = temp;
}

int x = 5, y = 10;
swap(x, y);  // x=10, y=5
```

---

### Reference as Return Value

```cpp
int& getElement(vector<int>& vec, int index) {
  return vec[index];  // Return reference to allow assignment
}

vector<int> data = {1, 2, 3};
getElement(data, 1) = 99;  // data[1] becomes 99
```

---

### Const Reference

```cpp
void print(const vector<int>& data) {
  for (int x : data) {
    cout << x << " ";
  }
}

print(data);  // Read-only, no copy
```

---

### Rvalue Reference (C++11)

```cpp
void process(int&& x) {  // Rvalue reference (&&)
  cout << "Moving: " << x;
}

process(42);  // Binds to rvalue
```

---

> **Key Point:** References are safer than pointers because they must be initialized and cannot be null. Use references for function parameters and return values when possible.

quiz.question: Can a reference be reassigned to refer to a different variable?
